steps.n: Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce this word of warning. This is a common exclamation in the Quran used to warn of severe consequences.

steps.m: woe/destruction (as a warning to هُمَزَةٍ لُّمَزَةٍ - the slanderer and backbiter)
steps.n: Added tanween damma to show this is the subject of the sentence. This word is warning those who slander and backbite.
This word is used to indicate totality or completeness
Added damma and doubled the middle letter to create a word meaning 'every' or 'all'

steps.m: of every/of all (describing هُمَزَةٍ لُّمَزَةٍ - slanderer and backbiter)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to هُمَزَةٍ لُّمَزَةٍ (slanderer and backbiter)
Added لِ at the beginning to mean 'for' or 'to'
Added shadda on the ل to emphasize the meaning

steps.m: to mock or insult others behind their back
steps.n: This root describes the action of mocking or slandering others
Added vowels to connect the letters together and show this happened in the past
Changed first vowel to damma and added ة to show this person mocks others as a habit
Added the ٍ mark because this word comes after لِكُلِّ (meaning 'for every')

steps.m: to mock or criticize others' faults
steps.n: This root describes the action of finding faults in others to mock them
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past-tense verb
Changed first vowel to damma and added ة to show this person does this action as a habit

steps.m: one who constantly mocks and finds faults
steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows this person does this action very frequently
Added tanwin kasra to show this is describing an indefinite person

steps.m: who/which (describes the person who جَمَعَ مَالًا - gathered wealth)
steps.n: This is the base form of the relative pronoun that connects descriptions to people or things. Here it introduces the description of someone who gathered wealth.

steps.m: The one who (describes the person who جَمَعَ مَالًا - gathered wealth)
steps.n: Added ٱلّ at the beginning to make it specific and definite, like saying 'the one' instead of just 'one'

steps.m: to gather or collect things together
he gathered (subject of the relative clause ٱلَّذِى)
steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. This word describes what the person mentioned in ٱلَّذِى (who/the one who) did.
Connected the letters together. The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two vowels. This word is what was collected in the phrase 'collected wealth'.
The added ـًا at the end shows this is 'a/some wealth' (not 'the wealth') and is being acted upon by the verb 'collected'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Doubling of middle root letter intensifies the meaning of counting

steps.m: he counted it over and over
steps.n: The هُ at the end shows what is being counted

steps.m: and he counted it over and over (referring to مَالًا/wealth)
steps.n: The و at the beginning means 'and'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make it a past tense verb

steps.m: he thinks/calculates (that مَالَهُۥٓ أَخْلَدَهُۥ - his wealth made him immortal)
steps.n: Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, sukun on first root letter, and ُ at end. In this ayah, it shows what the person wrongly thinks about their wealth.
Basic form of the connecting particle that joins two parts of a sentence

steps.m: that indeed (connects يَحْسَبُ 'he thinks' with what follows)
steps.n: Adding the shadda (doubled letter) and fatha marks makes this particle stronger, showing emphasis. It connects what someone thinks (يَحْسَبُ) with what they think about (their wealth)
The root letters م-و-ل form مال when the middle و changes to ا (alif) and gets a fatha mark, making it mean 'wealth'
Adding هُ makes it 'his wealth' - like adding 's in English
The small mark after هُ shows emphasis when reading
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Adding أَ at the start makes this verb causative - meaning someone is making the action happen to someone else
The هُۥ at the end shows who the action is happening to (him). This word is the object of يَحْسَبُ (he thinks), showing what is being thought.
This is the basic form of the word that means to stop or prevent something

steps.m: No indeed! (Used for strong denial or rejection)
steps.n: Adding ا at the end turns this into an emphatic particle that strongly rejects what came before it. In this ayah, it's rejecting the wrong actions mentioned in previous verses.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to passive voice by adding يُ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: he will surely be thrown away
steps.n: Adds لَ to emphasize that this will definitely happen

steps.m: he will most certainly be thrown away
steps.n: Added نَّ at the end for extra emphasis, showing this will definitely happen
Basic preposition showing location or position inside something
Added ى at the end to complete the word. This preposition connects to الْحُطَمَةِ (the Crusher) to show what someone will be thrown into.

steps.m: to crush or break into small pieces
steps.n: The root conveys the idea of crushing or breaking something completely
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed first vowel to damma and added ة to show this is something that crushes a lot
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific (like adding 'the' in English)

steps.m: the Crusher (a name of Hell, following فِى/in)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word فِى (in)
This is an interrogative particle used to ask questions
Added و at the beginning to connect this question with what came before
The long alif (آ) at the end adds emphasis to the question. This word is asking about what follows it (أَدْرَىٰكَ - made you know)
Basic meaning of knowing or understanding something
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make past tense verb
Adding أَ at start and sukun on first root letter makes it causative - making someone else know
Added alif maksura (ى with small alif above) at end as required for words ending in ى
Added كَ at end to show 'you' as the object receiving the action
This is a question word used to ask 'what is it?' about something unknown. In this ayah, it's asking about الْحُطَمَةُ (the Crusher).
Basic meaning of breaking or crushing something
Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past tense verb
Changed first vowel to damma and added ة to show something that does this action a lot
Added ال to make it specific - this refers to Hell, which crushes everything within it
Added the 'u' sound mark at the end to show this is the subject being talked about
The و (weak letter) changes to ا when connecting the letters with vowels. This is a common pattern for words about things related to the root meaning.
The ُ shows this is the subject being described by the word ٱلْمُوقَدَةُ (kindled/ignited) that comes after it.
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word in Arabic that always refers to The One True God.
Added kasra at the end to show possession, and shadda on ل. This word is connected to نَارُ (fire) showing that the fire belongs to Allah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he kindled' to 'something that was lit' by adding مُ at start (shows passive) and ة at end (shows feminine)
Added ٱلْ to show this is referring to a specific fire mentioned in نَارُ ٱللَّهِ (the Fire of Allah)
Added ُ to show this word is describing نَارُ (the Fire)
This is the basic feminine relative pronoun meaning 'that' or 'which'. The ي at the end is written as ى when it's at the end of a word.
Added ٱلَّ at the beginning to make it a definite relative pronoun. This word connects to تَطَّلِعُ (rises up) in the ayah, describing the fire that rises up.
The basic meaning involves something coming up or becoming visible
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb

steps.m: she rises up/emerges forcefully (onto الْأَفْـِٔدَةِ - the hearts)
steps.n: Added ت at start to show feminine subject, doubled ل with shadda to show intensity, changed vowels to show present tense. This describes how the fire forcefully rises up to reach the hearts.
This is the basic form showing the concept of being high or elevated
Adding ى at the end turns this into a preposition that shows one thing is above or over another. Here it shows the fire rises over the hearts.
This root is related to the heart and its functions
The pattern creates a noun that refers to the physical or spiritual heart
Changes from single 'heart' to plural 'hearts' by adding أَ at start and changing middle letters - used for small groups of things (3-10 hearts)
Added 'the' and kasra ending because it follows the word على (upon)
This is a particle that adds emphasis to what comes after it, like adding 'definitely' or 'certainly' in English
The هَا at the end refers back to the Fire (نَارُ) mentioned earlier, which is feminine in Arabic
This is a basic preposition showing something is on top of or affecting something else, like saying 'on' or 'upon' in English
The ى changes to يْ to prepare for attaching the next part. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.

steps.m: upon them (referring to the disbelievers mentioned earlier)
steps.n: Added هِم at the end to show who is being affected - in this case 'them' refers back to the disbelievers mentioned earlier in the surah

steps.m: to close or lock something tightly

steps.n: Added أ (alif) at start and vowels to make it mean 'he closed/locked' in the past
Changed to show something receiving the action instead of doing it
Added feminine marker to match what's being described (the Fire)
The ٌ (tanween) at the end shows this word is a subject or describes a subject
This is a basic preposition that shows location or containment
Added kasra (ِ) under ف to show how to pronounce it as 'fi'

steps.m: in (something is in عَمَدٍ مُّمَدَّدَةٍۭ - extended pillars)
steps.n: Changed the ي to ى which is a common spelling variation at the end of words. This word connects to عَمَدٍ مُّمَدَّدَةٍۭ (extended pillars) to show something is inside or within them.
Added fathas to connect the letters and form the plural noun for pillars

steps.m: pillars (possessed by/connected to something, in this case connected to مُّمَدَّدَةٍۭ - extended)
steps.n: The tanween kasra (ٍ) shows this word is indefinite (any pillars) and is being possessed by or connected to something else in the sentence
Connected the root letters and added a shadda to show intensity of the action
Added مُ at the beginning to show this is happening to something rather than someone doing it
Added ة to make it feminine, since it's describing عَمَدٍ (pillars) which is feminine
Added tanwin kasra (ٍ) to show this describes the pillars mentioned before it, and added shadda on first م to connect with previous word
